sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo.meaning: the relinquishment of all attachments; the giving up of every substratum of clinging
sabbūpadhipaṭinissaggo.summary: A variant spelling of sabbūpadhippaṭinissaggo; see that entry.

sabbūpadhippaṭinissaggo.meaning: the giving up of all attachments; forsaking and leaving behind every substratum of clinging
breakdown: Giving up, forsaking, relinquishment; sandhi doubling of p at the boundary.
sabbūpadhippaṭinissaggo.goenka: Sabbūpadhippaṭinissaggo appears in the canonical description of nibbāna: “this is peaceful, this is sublime — the calming of all saṅkhāra, the relinquishment of all attachment, the destruction of craving, dispassion, cessation, nibbāna.” The complete letting go of every clinging is not an idea to be believed but the culmination of practice, realised when the meditator ceases generating craving and aversion towards sensations.

sabhojano.meaning: with food; together with a meal

sabhāgasantati.meaning: continuity of similar (states); homogeneous continuity, lineage of like moments

sabhāsīlī.meaning: in the habit of frequenting assemblies; given to company
breakdown: Having the habit of (from sīla “habit, character”).
sabhāsīlī.note: uncertain: the lexicon's gloss line runs into the next entry (sabhojano “with food”); only “adhering to company” belongs to sabhāsīlī.

sabyañjanaṁ.meaning: with the letter; complete in phrasing, according to the letter (of the teaching)
sabyañjanaṁ.gram: adjective, accusative singular (often adverbial, paired with sātthaṁ “with the meaning”)

saccanulomika.meaning: in conformity with truth; consistent with the truth
breakdown: In direct order, conforming, going along with (anu + loma “with the grain”).

saccapaṭivedha.meaning: penetration of the truth; direct inner realization of the truths
saccapaṭivedha.goenka: Saccapaṭivedha is the penetration of truth by direct experience — the wisdom of bhāvanāmayā paññā, as distinct from accepting the truths by hearing or reasoning. The Four Noble Truths become liberating only when each is pierced through and realised within, at the level of one's own mind and body.

saccañāṇakiccañāṇakatañāṇasaṅkh.meaning: (fragment) named the knowledge of the truth, the knowledge of the task, and the knowledge of what has been done
saccañāṇakiccañāṇakatañāṇasaṅkh.gram: glossary artifact; line-broken fragment of the compound saccañāṇakiccañāṇakatañāṇasaṅkhātānaṁ
saccañāṇakiccañāṇakatañāṇasaṅkh.note: uncertain: the headword is a hyphenation fragment; see the entry saccañāṇakiccañāṇakatañāṇasaṅkhātānaṁ.

saccañāṇakiccañāṇakatañāṇasaṅkhātānaṁ.meaning: of (the three phases) named truth-knowledge, task-knowledge and done-knowledge
saccañāṇakiccañāṇakatañāṇasaṅkhātānaṁ.summary: Refers to the three rounds of knowledge applied to each Noble Truth in the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta: knowing the truth, knowing the task it requires, and knowing the task has been accomplished.
breakdown: What ought to be done, the task.

sacchikataṁ.gram: past participle of sacchikaroti, neuter/accusative singular
breakdown: With one's own eyes (sa + akkhi “eye”): direct witnessing.
Made, done (past participle of karoti): made directly seen, realized.

sacchikatvā.meaning: having fully realized; having directly experienced
breakdown: With one's own eyes: direct witnessing.
sacchikatvā.goenka: Sacchikatvā — “having realised for oneself, with direct experience” — carries the experiential emphasis at the heart of the teaching: the Dhamma is sandiṭṭhiko, to be seen here and now by each person within themselves, not accepted on another's authority.

sacchikātabbaṁ.meaning: should be realized; to be directly experienced
sacchikātabbaṁ.gram: future passive participle of sacchikaroti, neuter/accusative singular
breakdown: With one's own eyes: direct witnessing.
To be done (future passive participle of karoti): to be realized.

saccikaṭṭha.meaning: the real meaning; the highest truth, what is true in the ultimate sense
breakdown: Meaning, sense (attha; the vowels fuse at the boundary).

sadattho.meaning: the true welfare; one's own highest goal
breakdown: Good, true (sant > sat > sad by sandhi).

saddamanussāvesuṁ.meaning: they made the sound heard; they raised a cry (proclaimed aloud)
saddamanussāvesuṁ.gram: compound verb, aorist 3rd person plural (saddaṁ + anussāvesuṁ)
breakdown: Sound, cry (accusative); the niggahīta joins the following vowel as m.
They made heard, proclaimed (aorist causative of anu + √su “to hear”).
saddamanussāvesuṁ.note: divergence: the lexicon's breakdown reads sadda + manussā + avesuṁ (“sound + humankind + heard”); DPD parses saddaṁ + anussāvesi, “made the sound heard”, which fits the stock passage of the devas raising a cry.

saddavitakko.meaning: thought about sound; (initial) thought directed to sounds

saddhammassavanaṁ.meaning: hearing the true Dhamma; listening to the true teaching
breakdown: Good, true (sant > sat > sad by sandhi).
Hearing, listening; sandhi doubling of s at the boundary.

saddhivihāriko.meaning: co-resident; a fellow bhikkhu dwelling in the same monastery (a pupil living with his teacher)

saddhivihāriko fellow.meaning: a fellow bhikkhu dwelling in the same monastery
saddhivihāriko fellow.gram: glossary artifact; masculine noun saddhivihārika, nominative singular
saddhivihāriko fellow.note: uncertain: the headword is a run-on glossary line for saddhivihāriko; see that entry.

saddhiṁ together.note: uncertain: the headword is a run-on glossary line for saddhiṁ “together with”.

saddhā.goenka: Saddhā — confidence, devotion or faith — is described as the essential element with which one sets out on the path. In the tradition it is not blind belief: confidence in the Buddha, Dhamma and Saṅgha arises and matures through one's own experience of the practice, and in the chariot simile of the Brahmayāna passage saddhā is yoked together with paññā to draw the vehicle to liberation.

saddhāpaṭilābhena.meaning: through the gaining of faith; by the achievement of confidence

saddhāvīriyasādhanaṁ.meaning: accomplished through faith and effort; producing (its result) by confidence and energy

breakdown: Root associated with going, reaching; sagga is the sphere “gone to” by wholesome deeds.
sagga.goenka: Sagga is the general term for the spheres that exist above the human (manussā) world — the different fields of the devas and of Brahmā's retinue. Rebirth there is the fruit of wholesome kamma, but even these heavenly fields are impermanent and within the round of becoming; the goal of the path lies beyond them.

saggesu.meaning: in the heavens; in the heavenly fields

sahajātasīlādiguṇayogā.meaning: through being yoked to co-arisen qualities such as sīla; from association with the virtues born together (with the path moment)

sahassavaggo.meaning: the Sahassa Vagga (“Chapter of the Thousand”, e.g. in the Dhammapada)
sahassavaggo.gram: proper noun (chapter title), masculine nominative singular

sahasākārā.meaning: violent deeds; acts of violence (in the list: destruction, murder, binding, robbery, plundering and deeds of violence)
sahasākārā.gram: masculine noun, compound, nominative plural (or ablative singular)
sahasākārā.note: uncertain: the lexicon's breakdown covers the longer stock list chedana-vadha-bandhana-viparāmosa-ālopa-sahasākārā; only “violent deed” belongs to sahasākārā.

breakdown: Root meaning “to place”: placed together, joined.

sahita/sanhita.gram: past participle of sandahati (two spellings of the same form)
breakdown: Prefix “together” (appearing as san- in the variant spelling).

sahāvassa.meaning: simultaneously with (it); together with, at that very moment for him
breakdown: Just, even (the vowels fuse: sahā + va).

sahāvassa simultaneously.note: uncertain: the headword is a run-on glossary line for sahāvassa “simultaneously with”.

sajjhāyamakaruṁ.meaning: they made recitation; they performed the rehearsal (of the texts)
sajjhāyamakaruṁ.gram: compound verb phrase, aorist 3rd person plural: sajjhāyaṁ + akaruṁ
breakdown: Recitation, rehearsal, repetition (accusative); the niggahīta joins the following vowel as m.

sakadāgāmiphalaṁ.meaning: the fruit of once-returning (the second stage of liberation)
breakdown: Once-returner (sakid “once” + āgāmī “coming back”): one who will return to this world at most once more.

sakalamevidaṁ.meaning: this entire (thing); all of this, thus
breakdown: This; the vowels and niggahīta fuse across the boundaries.

sakammapasutā.meaning: intent on their own activities; busy with their own work

sakaṁyeva.meaning: their very own; just one's own
sakaṁyeva.gram: adjective saka, accusative singular + emphatic particle yeva
breakdown: Just, even (emphatic); a linking glide y appears at the boundary.
sakaṁyeva.note: uncertain: the lexicon's gloss line runs into the next entry (sakideva “only once”); only “their own + even so” belongs to sakaṁyeva.

sakhilā.gram: adjective sakhila, nominative plural (or feminine singular)

sakkacca.gram: indeclinable adverb (gerund of sakkaroti, sat + kacca)
